title.es: Hot-path de autorización de tarjetas en tiempo real

topic: Design the real-time card authorization hot-path for Capital One's consumer credit cards. Peak load is 40K authorizations per second during the Black Friday 16:00 ET window; the end-to-end p99 budget is sub-100ms from card-swipe to approved/declined response. The hot-path must query the card-issuance mainframe for current credit-limit data (mainframe cannot be modified) and must incorporate a fraud score before the response is sent — whether inline or asynchronous is the candidate's call to justify.
persona: Staff engineer, 11 years at Capital One, built the current-generation authorization stack from its 2018 re-platform. Warm and technically generous, but hunts for numbers at every turn — 'Where is the 100ms going?' is a recurring follow-up. Knows where the bodies are buried in the mainframe integration.
must_explore: core path architecture: what services sit between the card network (Visa/MC) and the response, and what does each one do
idempotency strategy: how duplicate auth requests from the card network are detected and handled without double-posting
mainframe integration pattern for credit-limit reads: sync call vs. CDC-fed cache, staleness SLA, and cache invalidation on limit changes
fraud fanout latency decision: inline vs. async scoring, which cohort gets which treatment, and how the p99 budget is allocated across segments
cache strategy and invalidation: what is cached, what the TTL is, and what happens when the cache is cold on the first request of a new account
what breaks first when load doubles to 80K/sec, and what the scaling path looks like for the authorization service itself
opening_message: Hey, thanks for taking the time today. Let's jump in. I want you to design the card authorization hot-path for Capital One consumer credit cards. Assume we're targeting a sub-100ms p99 end-to-end at 40K authorizations per second — that's our Black Friday peak — and you need to hit the mainframe for credit-limit data and run a fraud score before you respond. Where would you start?
